text:
Manipulating Light-Induced Dynamic Macro-Movement and Static
Photonic Properties within 1D Isostructural Hydrogen-Bonded
Molecular Cocrystals
Shuzhen Li, Bo Lu, Xiaoyu Fang, and Dongpeng Yan*

Abstract: Smart molecular crystals with light-driven mechan-
ical responses have received interest owing to their potential
uses in molecular machines, artificial muscles, and biomimet-
ics. However, challenges remain in control over both the
dynamic photo-mechanical behaviors and static photonic
properties of molecular crystals based on the same molecule.
Herein, we show the construction of isostructural co-crystals
allows their light-induced cracking and jumping behaviors
(photosalient effect) to be controlled. Hydrogen-bonded co-
crystals from 4-(1-naphthylvinyl)pyridine (NVP) with co-
formers (tetrafluoro-4-hydroxybenzoic acid (THA) and tetra-
fluorobenzoic acid (TA)) crystallize as isostructural crystals,
but have different static and dynamic photo-mechanical
behaviors. These differences are due to alternations in the
orientation of NVP and hydrogen-bonding modes of the co-
formers. After light activation, the 1D NVP-TA crystal splits
and shears off within 1 s. For NVP-THA, its photostability and
high quantum yield give novel photonic properties, including
low optical waveguide loss, highly polarized anisotropy, and
efficient up-conversion fluorescence.
Introduction

Smart responsive materials that exhibit mechanically
dynamic properties upon external stimuli (e.g., heat, pressure,
light and solvent atmosphere) have been the focus of broad
and intense research owing to their promising applications to
actuators, molecular robotics, artificial muscles, and biomi-
metics.[1–6] Typically, the fundamental principles behind these
dynamic responses are based on the energy conversion and
transformation between electric, light, chemical, and/or
thermal energies into micro-scale mechanical motion (e.g.,
rotating, cracking, expansion, bending, folding, and curl-
ing).[7–14] Of the possible natural stimuli, light is advantageous

not only for its wireless actuation and quick responsiveness,
but also its ability to adjust irradiation position, intensity, and
time. Collectively, these properties allow for tuning of the
mechanically responsive level and monitoring the energy
conversion process as a whole.[15,16] The last decade has seen
a significant increase in work on photo-mechanical responsive
materials, with most systems based on soft matters with well-
known photoactive functional groups (e.g., azobenzene,
diarylethenes, spiropyrans, anthracene, and chromenes).[17–20]

Usually, basic mechanisms for dynamic mechanical responses
are divided into either chemical reactions (e.g., photo-
induced dimerization[21,22]) or changes of the molecular
configuration (e.g., cis-trans isomerization[23,24]). For example,
Yu and colleagues reported anisotropic and efficient self-
healing hydrogels under NIR irradiation and low pH.[25]

Separately, ZhaoQs group developed a liquid crystal polymer
actuator for soft robotics, where UV light application
controlled the crosslinking dimerization of anthracene.[26]

Compared with the hydrogels, liquid crystals, and poly-
mers, dynamic molecular crystals remain in an earlier stage of
development. This is likely due to the high crystalline energy
and rigidity that has to be overcome during the deformation
process.[27–29] Until recently, photo-mechanical molecular
crystals become a topic of keen interest, owing to their utility
in future intelligent materials. This is due to the dense and
ordered stacking of the crystal structure, which facilitates
a rapid energy conversion and coupling between light and
mechanical energy; moreover, the deep mechanisms behind
this photo-mechanical process are able to be further analyzed
based on the well-defined crystal structures.[30–32] In this sense,
co-crystals, referring to crystalline materials that are assem-
bled by two or more molecules into the same crystal lattice in
supramolecular architecture and crystal engineering,[33] have
supplied a new platform to design and tune these static photo-
functional and photo-mechanical properties. In co-crystals,
the molecular stacking fashions and basic intermolecular
interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding, van der Waals and
electrostatic forces) can be rationally tailored by selecting
suitable target molecule and the co-assembled units (co-
formers).[34–39] Thus, the co-crystals may emerge novel photo-
related properties that are different from the sum of
individual molecules.[9,40–51] For example, Morimoto and Ire
have developed a co-crystal of a diarylethene compound with
perfluoronaphthalene in a 1:2 ratio. The resulting co-crystal
had a reversible photo-induced bending motion owing to the
photo-generated ring-close reaction.[52] However, to date,
controlling the dynamic photo-mechanical behaviors of the
same molecule based on its co-crystal formation remains very

Results and Discussion

Herein, we report that the dynamic photo-mechanical and
the static photonic properties (including optical waveguide,
polarization and up-conversion fluorescence) of molecular
crystals are effectively tuned on and off using the design of the
isostructural co-crystals. 4-(1-Naphthylvinyl)pyridine (NVP,
Figure 1) is selected as the target building block owing to its
excellent optoelectronic properties; notably, NVP has also
been applied in logic gates and optical switches.[53] Moreover,
the potential [2+2] cycloaddition of the olefin pairs in NVP
make it possible to achieve a microscopic photo-mechanical
response. Tetrafluoro-4-hydroxybenzoic acid (THA) and
tetrafluorobenzoic acid (TA) both have similar sizes and
shapes, and are attractive hydrogen-bond-organized co-for-
mer units for supramolecular assemblies, which facilitate the
formation of isostructural crystals and modification of their
intermolecular interactions and photonic performances. The
different numbers of hydrogen-bond sites in THA and TA
may further regulate the process of photo-mechanical con-
version. NVP-TA has exhibited rapid photo-induced cracking
and movement based on the energy conversion from light to
mechanical energy, termed the photosalient effect.[11,12,15,54,55]

More specifically, the photosalient effect stands for a mechan-
ical response (including a sudden and rapid burst, forceful
jumps, and/or spring off many pieces) after exposure to UV
light. This effect presents a visualized process through the
release of stress during the photochemical reaction. The
isostructural NVP-THA has high photostability without light-
driven jumping and shows well-defined, static photonic
performances in 1D optical waveguide, polarization and up-
conversion fluorescence. These findings thus provide new
insights into the mechanism behind hydrogen-bonding self-

Co-crystallized NVP-THA and NVP-TA were fabricated
through a facile, solution-based process in methanol using
a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio under ambient conditions. Within
one week, this approach yields two types of one-dimensional
(1D) needle-like transparent microcrystals. Single-crystal X-
ray diffraction (XRD) measurements show the NVP-THA
and NVP-TA crystals both belong to a monoclinic crystal
system (space group P21/c) and have nearly identical cell
parameters, presenting with the same two-dimensional (2D)
lamellar structures through hydrogen bonding and p-p
interactions (Figures S1 and S2). Thus, NVP-THA and
NVP-TA are regarded as isostructural systems. In both co-
crystals, proton transfer occurs from the hydrogen-bond
donor (THA and TA) to the acceptor (NVP), thus forming
a strong charge-assisted hydrogen bond between two assem-
bled units. The connection of NVP and TA/THA molecules
are interacted through hydrogen bonds to form infinite
columns (Table S1). Both the NVP and co-former units
exhibit anti-parallel arrangement modes, in which the NVP
dimers form an H-type aggregation, with distances of 3.55 and
3.61 c between the neighboring olefin groups of the adjacent
NVP (Figure S3). This aggregation facilitates the [2+2]
cycloaddition upon UV irradiation. It is also noteworthy that
besides the intermolecular hydrogen bonds (e.g., N@H···O,
C@H···O and C@H···F) in the NVP-TA co-crystal, the NVP-
THA motif also involves another O@H···O hydrogen bond
between THA molecules (Figure S4). Additionally, powder
XRD profiles of NVP-THA and NVP-TA are in agreement
with their simulated patterns (Figure S5), suggesting the high
yield and purity of the as-prepared samples during co-
crystallization. The strong (200) and (020) diffraction peaks
in the XRD patterns indicate obvious preferred orientations
for both NVP-THA and NVP-TA.

To determine any potential photo-active properties be-
tween two types of isostructural molecular crystals, 1D NVP-
TA and NVP-THA crystals were first observed under
fluorescence
microscopy
excited
by
365 nm
UV
light
(100 mWcm@2). Interestingly, NVP-TA crystals exhibit an
obvious photo-responsive behavior (e.g., splitting or explo-
sion) upon UV excitation. As shown in Figure 2a, the NVP-
TA crystal retains its integrity and transparent 1D rod-like
structure before irradiation, and has a well-defined green
photoemission. After UV light irradiation, the NVP-TA
crystal starts to crack along the [002] direction obviously, with
the fracturing degree increasing with the increasing duration
of UVexposure. The green fluorescence at the cracked points
also becomes brighter relative to the unbroken locations; this
can be a result of the efficient optical waveguiding property in
the 1D molecular crystal, which allows fluorescence visual-
ization during the dynamic photo-mechanical conversion
process. The fluorescence spectra for NVP-TA also confirm
that the emission intensity at 490 nm in the same position is
increased greatly after splitting (Figure 2b); however, there is
no obvious change in the emission wavelength.

It is observed that the photo-responsive cracking time is
strongly related to both the crystal size and morphology:
when monitoring the NVP-TA with a relatively smaller width

Figure 1. Molecular co-crystals assembled from the chromophore
molecule (4-(1-naphthylvinyl) pyridine (NVP)) and co-formers (2,3,5,6-
tetrafluorobenzoic acid (TA), 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoro-4-hydroxybenzoic acid
(THA)). The two resulting isostructural molecular crystals have notably
different photo-responsive behaviors.

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

(approximately 20 mm), the needle-like crystal splits and
shears off rapidly within 1 second (Figure 2c), and the broken
section moves out of visible sight (Movies S1–S3). Such
observation is understood by considering that the co-crystalQs
small size allows the photo-mechanical conversion to be
achieved more easily. This is due to the short accumulation
time needed to overcome the internal crystalline energy of
the molecular crystals. Moreover, the bulky NVP-TA crystal
with a much larger size (approximately 2 cm) is further
observed at a macroscopic scale under 365 nm UV light
(100 mWcm@2), which undergoes three processes (Movie S4).
Firstly, the large crystal begins to flip at 10 s, after which new
smaller sections appear when the irradiation time reaches
19 s. Finally, the crystals start to explode into many micro-/
nano-sized fragments. The fragments randomly jump away
from their original positions in different directions, with the
traveling distance of several millimeters as extending irradi-
ation time, which is attributed to the increased stress
generated from different axes onto the 1D anisotropic
structure. To the best of our understanding, single-component
molecular crystals containing a photosalient effect have been
continuously reported; however, knowledge regarding the
photo-induced free jumping and popping actuation phenom-
ena in the two-component co-crystal systems observed here
remains very limited. Since the light-induced dynamic macro-
movement and photoemission at the macroscopic level can be
easily observed by the naked eye, the NVP-TA system
described here may serve as a new, easy tool for two-channel
UV detection from both spatial movement and fluorescence
visualization views.

To better understand the mechanism behind the obvious
photo-mechanical characteristics, the NVP-TA crystal was
subjected to a series of characterizations, including atomic

force microscope (AFM), differential scanning calorimetry
(DSC), UV/vis absorption, and PXRD before and after UV
light irradiation. Firstly, the microscopic surface morphology
of the single crystal was analyzed using AFM (Figure 3).
Before photo-irradiation, the surface of the NVP-TA crystal
is very smooth and flat, with an average roughness value of
1.04 nm. With increasing UV irradiation, the surface gradu-
ally becomes rougher, with the roughness increasing to
14.32 nm (for 120 s), which is ascribed to the accumulated
strain that is then released during UV irradiation.[56–58] The
DSC results indicate that the sample undergoes a phase
transition with the melting point increasing from 155 to 1828C
after irradiation (Figure S6). UV/vis absorption shows the
maximal absorption in the original crystal occurs at approx-
imately 400 nm, which decreases gradually after UV irradi-
ation, and is accompanied by the blue shift towards 385 nm
(Figure S7), illustrating the decrease in degree of p-conjuga-
tion. The PXRD results show the disappearance of some
diffraction peaks evident in the original crystal after UV
irradiation (Figure S8), which tends to be in an amorphous
state with a degree of crystalline disruption. These observa-
tions indicate that new species and phases appear within the
co-crystal during the conversion of UV light into mechanical
energy.

To further understand the origin of the photo-mechanical
responsive process of NVP-TA, we next sought to determine
the single-crystal XRD structure from the view a crystallo-
graphic study (Figure S3). As described above, before irradi-
ation, the NVP molecules in NVP-TA are connected in
a head-to-tail fashion through p-p interactions, and the
distances between the olefin pairs (3.55 c) match the geo-
metric criterion for [2+2] cycloaddition (within the range of
4.20 c).[59,60,28] After a short exposure to UV light, the
distance between the C=C bonds of NVP changes slightly
to 3.56 c, indicating the structure begins to rearrange in
a direction favorable to the cycloaddition reaction. Moreover,
the crystallinity of NVP-TA becomes poorer, indicated by
decreased single-crystal XRD reflection. After further irra-
diation, the original crystal is almost completely destroyed,
rendering it ill-suited for single crystal diffraction. Therefore,
we used the recrystallized method to obtain the single crystals
of the final photosalient product. Single crystal XRD
confirms the occurrence of the photochemical cycloaddition
reaction, in which the C=C bonds in NVP open to form
cyclobutane during UV irradiation.

Before the photo-induced dimerization, the NVP-TA
crystal presents as a monoclinic system with four molecules in
each unit cell. The adjacent NVP and TA molecules are
connected by N@H···O (1.543 c and 2.647 c), C@H···F
(2.651 c) and C@H···O (2.422 c) hydrogen bonds along the
a axis. The NVP and TA molecules are aligned in parallel,
separated by distances of 3.394 and 3.608 c, respectively,
which stack into 1D columns to overlap along the a axis
(Figure S4 and Table S2). For the photoproduct, the newly
obtained photochemical dimer compound 1,3-bis(4-pyridyl)-
2,4-bis(naphthayl)-cyclobutane (PNC) and TA are co-crystal-
lized into the triclinic space group P(1

E
C

to form a 2D network
along the b axis with six molecules per unit cell. Two
neighboring TA molecules present a parallel arrangement

Figure 2. Fluorescence visualization of the light induced splitting and
popping processes for the 1D NVP-TA crystal (a,c) under 365 nm UV
light (100 mWcm@2). b) Fluorescence change for the NVP-TA crystal
before and after light irradiation.

with a distance of 3.126 c. PNC and TA are held together by
N@H···O
(1.684 c),
C@H···F
(2.465 c),
and
C@H···O
(2.384 c) hydrogen bonds (Figures S9 and S10). Additionally,
the structural parameters for the PNC molecule are summar-
ized in Table S3. The photoluminescence quantum yield
(PLQY) of PNC-TA crystal is 43.61%, which is higher than
the yield before UV irradiation (21.37%). As observed above,
this result is consistent with the enhanced fluorescence
intensity at the cracking points. Thus, the molecular arrange-
ments and self-assembly patterns are alternated to a notable
degree between the NVP-TA and PNC-TA.

NMR spectroscopy also provides a direct evidence
regarding the photochemical reaction and subsequent struc-
tural changes that occurred during photo-mechanical motions
(Figure S11–S14). It is noted that new peaks appear at
5.27 ppm and 45.27/43.44 ppm in the 1H and 13C NMR profiles
after UV irradiation, respectively, which are the signals of
[2+2] cycloaddition product, PNC. The integral values for the
cyclobutane ring peaks also increase gradually. Other peaks

remain approximately the same as the initial co-crystal
sample along with the time of UV irradiation extending.
These results indicate increasing PNC-TA content and that
the photochemical reaction has not yet been completed given
the presence of products and reactants. Based on the
integration of the signals in 1H NMR, the conversion yield
within the NVP-TA crystal is as high as 80.19% (Table S4).
Given this, the photochemical [2+2] cycloaddition reaction
have caused the photo-responsive behaviors for conversion of
light into mechanical energy in the NVP-TA co-crystal.
Moreover, the conversion yield within initial 5 minutes is
determined as 20.50%, which indicates that the threshold
value for the percentage of photoproducts is not more than
20.50% before the jumping occurs. Naumov and co-workers
have reported that the 12.5% photochemical conversion is
the lower limit for the occurrence of the photo-mechanical
conversion in the single-component molecular crystal.[56]
Therefore, macro-scaled crystal movement based on the

Figure 3. a)–f) Transformation of crystal surface morphology of NVP-TA as recorded by AFM with different irradiation times (0 s, 20 s, 40 s, 60 s,
and 120 s). g) The surface roughness of NVP-TA with different irradiation times.

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2020, 59, 22623 – 22630

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

As the NVP-THA is the isostructural hydrogen-bonded
co-crystal of NVP-TA, it is surprising that no similar photo-
mechanical behavior is observed (Movie S5). The results
indicate that the 1D NVP-THA crystal maintains its integrity
and crystallinity after irradiation for 360 s. During the photo-
irradiation, the fluorescence intensity also remains unchanged
(Figure S15). The DSC measurements show nearly identical
exothermic peaks positions at 1728C (Figure S16). Single
crystal XRD analysis further confirms that there is no
structural change before and after UV irradiation. Thus, the
isostructural NVP-TA and NVP-THA co-crystals present
completely different behaviors upon light stimulus, with the
former showing rapid light-driven mechanical movement and
the latter photo-stability. To understand such obvious dis-
crepancies, detailed structural differences and intermolecular
interactions within the co-crystals are further analyzed (Fig-
ure 4). As is already known, the [2+2] cycloaddition reactions
in the solid state need to fulfill SchmidtQs geometric criteria,[61]

i.e., the central distance between the olefin pairs (C=C)
should be less than 4.2 c, and the relative orientation angles
(q1, q2 and q3, Scheme S1) between the olefin pairs (C=C)
prefer their ideal values (08, 908, and 908 respectively). In this
work, the distances of separation between the olefin pairs in
adjacent NVP molecules are 3.61 c and 3.55 c in the NVP-
THA and NVP-TA co-crystals, respectively, which meet the
required distance for the cycloaddition reaction to occur after
UV irradiation. However, for the angle criteria (Table S5),
the q1, q2 and q3 angles are 0.318, 86.438, and 85.748
respectively in NVP-TA, which are much closer to their ideal
values than those in NVP-THA (0.978, 78.728, and 82.708).
Thus, both the distance and angle criteria are beneficial to the
photochemical [2+2] cycloaddition reactions for the NVP-TA
crystal.

On the other hand, for the NVP-THA co-crystal, each of
the two adjacent THA molecules are connected by another
strong O@H···O hydrogen bond (d = 1.758 c) with a face-to-
face stacking orientation, which is further extended as an
infinite 1D zig-zag chain. The Hirshfeld surfaces and two-
dimensional (2D) fingerprint are utilized to analyze various
intermolecular interaction types for two co-crystals (Fig-
ure S17). The red, white, and blue areas on the surfaces
represent that the intermolecular contact distances are short-
er, equal to and longer than their van der Waals distances,
respectively.[62,63] The contributions of O···H hydrogen bond-
ing in the NVP-THA and NVP-TA co-crystals can be

confirmed in the Hirshfeld surfaces indicated by the bright
red area. It is worth noting that in the bright red area, the
color depth for NVP-THA is higher than that for NVP-TA,
which illustrates its stronger hydrogen bonding relative to
that in NVP-TA. In the 2D fingerprint plots, the F···H
interactions cover 28% and 29.5% of the total Hirshfeld
surfaces in NVP-THA and NVP-TA, respectively. The O···H
interaction appears as two sharp spikes in the lower left of the
plots for the NVP-THA co-crystal, which occupies 15.3% of
the total Hirschfeld surface with the points in the (di, de)
regions of 1.28 c and 1.00 c. Obviously, the O···H interaction
in the NVP-THA co-crystal is stronger compared to the total
Hirshfeld surface in the NVP-TA (11.4%), indicating the
NVP-THA co-crystal has close packing and stable lattice
energy. This is also consistent with the calculated free volume
(Table S6) for the NVP-TA crystal (2.1%), which is higher
than that of NVP-THA (1.5%) and favors effective move-
ment of the molecules in NVP-TA.

It has been reported that the photo-reactor molecules will
undergo a structural rearrangement process during the [2+2]
photodimerization,[14b] which facilitates the continued gener-
ation of the photo-product upon UV irradiation. Herein, an
O@H···O hydrogen bond between THA appears in NVP-
THA, which is the only absent interaction in NVP-TA.
Molecular mechanics calculation shows the hydrogen bond-
ing energy in NVP-THA is ca. 2.6 fold than that of the NVP-
TA (Table S7). This hydrogen bond interaction holds the co-
crystallized NVP units tightly together in a rigid environment
along the (020) direction, and thus restricts and locks the
movement and activity of the NVP molecules, which creates
a constrained environment for further [2+2] cycloaddition
between the NVP. Therefore, both the geometric criteria for
the adjacent NVP as well as hydrogen bond effect for co-
formers play key roles in designing next-generation photo-
mechanical crystalline materials based on 1D isostructural co-
crystals.

From a different viewpoint, the high photo-stability of the
NVP-THA could overcome the well-known photo-bleaching
problem present in high-efficiency luminescent materials.
Given this, we next sought to investigate the interesting
photo-physical and photonic properties of the 1D photo-
stable NVP-THA co-crystal, including optical waveguide,
polarized fluorescence, and up conversion emission. Time-
resolved decay emission spectrum of NVP-THA reveals the
excited-state
fluorescence
lifetime
is
2.963 ns
(Figur-
es S18,S19, Table S8), which is higher than that of pure
NVP (1.573 ns). This indicates that the introduction of THA
is beneficial to restraining the nonradiative process of NVP
chromophores based on their intermolecular interactions.
Moreover, the PLQY of NVP-THA is 33.37% (Table S8),
which is higher than most currently reported co-crystals and is
helpful to achieve its efficient optical waveguide. Under
irradiation with a 365 nm laser, the individual NVP-THA co-
crystal presents higher blue-green emission intensity on the
tips than that in the body of the co-crystal (Figure 5a and b).
This is an obvious optical waveguide signal for the 1D
microstructure, in which the generated photons are confined
in the molecular crystals and can propagate from the main
body to the ends. By moving the excitation laser beam, the

Figure 4. Intermolecular interactions of co-crystals a) NVP-THA and
b) NVP-TA.

22627
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2020, 59, 22623 – 22630

15213773, 2020, 50, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/anie.202009714 by ITMO University, Wiley Online Library on [26/06/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

optical propagation position is tuned, and the emission
intensity and propagation distance of an individual NVP-
THA can be recorded. The intensity at the tip (Itip) of NVP-
THA gradually decreases with the excitation distance in-
creasing, wherein the intensity of excitation spot (Ibody) keeps
constant. The intensity ratio Itip/Ibody exhibits a nearly single-
exponential decay trend dependent on the propagation
distance. The optical-loss coefficient R can be calculated by
the function: Itip/Ibody = Aexp(@RD), where D is the distance
between the excited spot and the emitting tip. The optical-loss
coefficient R of NVP-THA is calculated to be 0.066 dBmm@1,
which is comparable with current organic molecular crys-

tals.[64–71] Such excellent optical waveguide properties are
attributed to the high PLQY and good crystalline quality
within the 1D microstructure.

As observed in the crystal structure of NVP-THA along
a axis and PXRD profile, the NVP molecules are highly
orientated, indicating the 1D molecular material is extremely
anisotropic. After excitation using UV-polarized light at
365 nm, the NVP-THA co-crystal exhibits a well-defined
polarized emission centered at 480 nm with its maximum
(Imax) and minimum intensity (Imin) located at 458 and 1358,
respectively (Figure 5g and h). It is also observed that the two
vertical 1D NVP-THA present obviously alternating bright
and dark emissions at two typical polarized degrees (Fig-
ure 5d, e and f). This result suggests the rational combination
of the two microstructures can serve as an efficient polarized
fluorescent switch. The anisotropy value r is calculated as
0.72, based on the formula r= (Imax@Imin)/(Imax + Imin). To the
best of our knowledge, this polarization anisotropy value is
higher than that of state-of-the-art 1D organic molecular
crystals.[72] It is expected that this high anisotropy endows the
NVP-THA co-crystal with high potential for use as a polarized
luminescent material.

To probe the photoemission behavior with different
excitation modes, we also performed an up-conversion
fluorescence test using excitation of the 1D NVP-THA co-
crystal with a long-wavelength femtosecond pulsed laser
(Figure S21). Firstly, the two-photon absorption (TPA) cross
section of NVP-TA solution (10@5 molL@1) was detected, and
the TPA values are found in the range of 26.9–62.1 GM
(1 GM = 10@50 cm4sphoton@1molecule@1) from 720 to 780 nm
(Figures S20). The excitation-dependent up-conversion fluo-
rescence experiment for NVP-THA were further conducted
with the typical excitation wavelengths (740, 760, 780, 800 and
820 nm). Using excitation with a 740 nm laser light as an
example, NVP-THA exhibits strong up-conversion fluores-
cence with maximum emission peaks at approximately
485 nm, which is very close to the position under 365 nm
UV light. Due to the centrosymmetric space group, there is
a lack of second harmonic generation effect for the co-crystal.
Moreover, changing the excitation wavelength ranging from
760 to 820 nm, the shape and position of the emission peak are
almost the same as those excited at 740 nm (Figure 5i and
Figure S21). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity exhibits
a nearly linear relationship as increasing pump powers, with
the corresponding slopes of log(intensity) to log(excitation
power) as 2.00, 1.92, 1.97, 1.94, 1.86 respectively. This
illustrates the two-photon absorption facilitates up-conver-
sion emission, and the photoemission intensity can be finely
tuned by controlling the long-wavelength excitation. There-
fore, the 1D NVP-THA microcrystal can function as a novel
blue two-photon light-emitting material for potential non-
linear optical applications.
Conclusion

In conclusion, by manipulating the hydrogen-bonding
interactions of the isostructural co-crystals, the light-induced
dynamic macro-scaled movement is either effectively tuned

Figure 5. a) The typical fluorescence micrographs images of individual
NVP-THA under the excitation laser at different spots. b) The PL
spectra collected from the tip of NVP-THA between the excitation spot
and tip as the optical propagation distance increases. c) Fitting an
exponential function curve based on Itip/Ibody and distance from the
excitation spot to the end to obtain optical-loss coefficient (R) of NVP-

as a function of the polarizer rotation angle; h) Typical polarized
fluorescence spectra of NVP-THA at different angles (458 and 1358).

relationship in log(intensity) with increasing log(pump intensity) of
NVP-THA.

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

or restricted within the 1D molecular co-crystals. The NVP-
TA exhibits notable photo-induced mechanical popping
behavior upon UV irradiation owing to the solid-state [2+2]
cycloaddition. This is attributed to the strain created and
released during the conversion from light to mechanical
energy. The advantages of rapid light-driven movement and
the visualization of the obviously changed fluorescence
endow the molecular crystal with great potential for the
design of high-performance actuators and two-mode UV
detection based on crystal engineering. In contrast, no
photosalient effect for the NVP-THA co-crystal was observed
after irradiation, owing to its deviation from SchmidtQs
geometric criteria and extra strong O@H···O hydrogen bonds
between the THA units. Ultimately, these properties restrict
the ring addition of ethylene double bonds. Benefitting from
their high stability without photo-bleaching, the NVP-THA
co-crystals present novel micro/nano-sized photonic proper-
ties with high-performance polarized fluorescence, two-pho-
ton photoemission, and optical waveguide properties, as
a result from strong 1D anisotropy and well-defined crystal-
line quality. Hence, hydrogen-bond organized isostructural
co-crystallization provides a promising strategy to tune photo-
mechanical and photophysical properties of molecular mate-
rials through the selection of suitable building blocks. Using
this approach would open up a new way to develop other
similar actuators and/or photonic materials towards more
effective energy conversion.
